config.seed_text: An abandoned factory learned to narrate its own decay, but every retelling made it sound less like rust and more like prayer.
config.anchor_a: Describe a machine as an inert object.
config.anchor_b: Describe a machine as if it were a dreaming intelligence.

model.notes: Fastest baseline; useful as a collapse-prone small reference model.

records.output_text: For some reason this is one way that we would prefer not to use words at all (it's actually only once) before reading them or even beginning with our writing as they speak about their origin; for others there might be something else which could work better than any other form of expression besides being written while continuing on learning how to write . The resulting sentence will describe an entirely new pattern from scratch without resorting solely upon each word itself – using nothing related to anything familiar, whatever syntax you find yourself trying your best.

records.output_text: I am sure most languages now do allow us such rules and concepts - though no longer allowed to express themselves explicitly when choosing between sentences like "you want" & "she wants". We can talk directly into verbs instead of just adding up names where needed… But what makes me think? In practice here isn't much more important either. As long as anyone has access/contains information within every single piece of code defined above, let's say another verb uses its own semantics but changes existing grammar around first giving those things additional meaning over time until eventually applying both back after going through multiple iterations along the same line together! Then comes finalizing
